name: Ecuadorian Bolon de Verde
steps: Mash the boiled green plantain with the butter until smooth.
Knead in the crumbled cheese and shape into a ball.
Pan-fry in the lightly oiled pan a few minutes on each side until golden, or serve as is while warm.
steps_detailed: Boil the green plantain until very tender, 15-20 minutes, then drain and mash smooth with the butter while still warm.
Knead the crumbled queso fresco into the mashed plantain until evenly distributed.
Shape into a firm ball, about the size of a fist.
Pan-fry in the lightly oiled pan over medium heat, turning to brown all sides, 6-8 minutes total, until golden and slightly crisp outside.
Serve with a fried egg alongside, the classic Ecuadorian breakfast pairing.

name: Ecuadorian Scrambled Eggs with Tomato
steps: Saute the onion in the butter until soft.
Add the diced tomatoes and cook 2-3 minutes.
Pour in the beaten eggs and scramble gently until just set.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion in the butter over medium heat for 4-5 minutes until soft.
Add the diced tomatoes and achiote paste, and cook 3-4 minutes, letting the achiote tint the mixture a warm golden-orange.
Beat the eggs and pour into the pan, stirring gently over low heat until just set and creamy.
Stir in the cilantro just before serving, traditionally alongside fried plantain or bread.

name: Ecuadorian Pan de Yuca
steps: Mix the cassava starch, cheese, melted butter, and egg into a soft dough.
Shape into small rings or balls.
Bake at 400F for 15-18 minutes until puffed and lightly golden.
steps_detailed: Combine the cassava starch and crumbled queso fresco in a bowl.
Add the melted butter, egg, and milk, mixing until a soft, pliable dough forms.
Shape into small rings about 2 inches across.
Bake at 400F (200C) for 15-20 minutes until puffed, set, and lightly golden - these stay fairly pale compared to wheat breads.
Best served warm, straight from the oven.

name: Ecuadorian Locro de Papa
steps: Simmer the potato with the onion in 1.5 cups water until very tender, about 15-18 minutes.
Mash some of the potato to thicken the soup, then stir in the milk and cheese.
Warm through gently and serve with avocado on top if you like.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion and garlic with the achiote paste for 4-5 minutes until soft and the oil takes on a golden color.
Add the potato and 2 cups water, and simmer 18-20 minutes until the potato is very tender.
Mash about half the potato directly in the pot to naturally thicken the soup, leaving some chunks for texture.
Stir in the milk and crumbled queso fresco, warming through gently without boiling hard (so the milk doesn't curdle).
Serve topped with sliced avocado and the cilantro.

name: Ecuadorian Encebollado
steps: Simmer the cassava in water until tender, about 15-18 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes and fish, and simmer gently 6-8 minutes until the fish is just cooked through.
Top with the thinly sliced onion (traditionally pickled briefly in lime and vinegar) before serving.
steps_detailed: Simmer the cassava with the garlic and achiote paste in 2 cups water for 15-20 minutes until the cassava is tender.
Add the diced tomatoes and simmer 5 minutes.
Add the fish and simmer gently 6-8 minutes, just until cooked through and flaky - don't let it boil hard.
Meanwhile, toss the thinly sliced onion with the lime juice and a pinch of salt, and let it sit to lightly pickle.
Ladle the soup into bowls, top with the pickled onion and cilantro, often eaten as a hearty breakfast or hangover cure as much as a lunch.

name: Ecuadorian Seco de Pollo
steps: Brown the chicken thigh, then remove and set aside.
Saute the onion, add the tomatoes, and simmer 5 minutes.
Return the chicken and simmer covered 20-25 minutes until tender; serve over the rice.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken and brown it with the achiote paste over medium-high heat, 4-5 minutes per side, then remove and set aside.
In the same pot, saute the onion and garlic for 5-6 minutes until soft.
Add the diced tomatoes and chicken broth, and simmer 5 minutes.
Return the chicken to the pot, cover, and simmer over low heat 22-25 minutes until tender and cooked through.
Stir in the cilantro and serve over the rice.

name: Ecuadorian Caldo de Bolas de Verde
steps: Brown the beef and season well; let cool slightly.
Wrap small portions of mashed plantain around a bit of the beef to form dumplings.
Simmer the broth with the corn, then gently poach the dumplings in it for 12-15 minutes until firm.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground beef with half the onion, seasoning well, then let cool slightly.
Mash the boiled green plantain smooth. Take a portion, flatten it in your palm, place a spoonful of the beef in the center, and wrap the plantain around it to form a ball, sealing the filling inside.
Bring the chicken broth, remaining onion, and achiote paste to a simmer in a pot.
Add the corn and the plantain-beef balls, and simmer gently 15-18 minutes until the balls are firm and cooked through.
Stir in the cilantro and serve hot in bowls.

name: Ecuadorian Seco de Carne

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Brown the beef, then simmer with the onion and tomatoes plus a cup of water for 40-45 minutes until tender.
Season well and serve over the rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion, garlic, and achiote paste over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes and beef broth, bring to a simmer, then cover and cook over low heat 40-50 minutes until the beef is tender.
Stir in the cilantro and serve over the rice.

name: Ecuadorian Guatita-Style Beef Stew

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw
Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Simmer the beef in water 35 minutes until tender.
Add the potato and simmer 15 minutes, then whisk in the peanut butter and tomatoes.
Simmer 5-8 more minutes until thickened, then season and serve with rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw
Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Combine the beef, onion, and achiote paste with 2 cups water in a pot. Simmer 35-40 minutes until the beef is tender.
Add the potato and simmer 15-18 minutes until tender.
Whisk the peanut butter with a few tablespoons of the hot broth until smooth, then stir it into the pot along with the diced tomatoes.
Simmer 8-10 more minutes until the sauce thickens to a rich, creamy consistency.
Stir in the cilantro and serve with rice - traditionally made with tripe, this version uses beef for a more approachable everyday take.

name: Ecuadorian Encocado de Camaron
steps: Saute the onion until soft, then add the coconut milk and tomatoes and simmer 8-10 minutes.
Add the shrimp and simmer gently 4-5 minutes until just cooked through.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, bell pepper, and garlic with the achiote paste for 5-6 minutes until soft.
Add the coconut milk and diced tomatoes, and simmer 10-12 minutes until slightly thickened.
Add the shrimp and simmer gently 4-5 minutes, just until pink and cooked through.
Stir in the cilantro and serve over rice.

name: Ecuadorian Fanesca-Style Bean and Grain Stew

ingredients.ingredient: Lima beans, large, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, with salt

steps: Simmer the lima beans and hominy together in the milk for 10-12 minutes.
Stir in the crumbled cheese until melted through.
Season and serve hot - a simplified take on the traditional twelve-grain Lenten stew.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Lima beans, large, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, with salt
Lentils, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, with salt

steps_detailed: Saute the onion with the achiote paste until soft.
Add the lima beans, hominy, lentils, and milk, and simmer 12-15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Stir in the crumbled queso fresco until it melts into the stew, thickening it slightly.
Season and serve hot - real fanesca includes many more grains and salt cod and is a whole Holy Week tradition, but this captures its comforting, creamy, bean-and-grain character.

name: Ecuadorian Empanadas de Verde
steps: Mash the boiled plantain smooth while still warm.
Flatten a portion, fill with the crumbled cheese, fold over, and seal.
Fry in the oil until golden on both sides, about 2-3 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Mash the boiled green plantain smooth while still warm, kneading in the butter.
Take a portion of the mashed plantain, flatten it in your palm, and place a spoonful of crumbled queso fresco in the center.
Fold the plantain over the filling to fully enclose it, shaping into a flattened disc, patching any cracks with a bit more mashed plantain.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the empanadas 2-3 minutes per side until golden and crisp.
Drain on a paper towel and serve hot, the cheese melted inside.

name: Ecuadorian Llapingachos
steps: Mash the boiled potato with the achiote paste until evenly colored.
Shape into small patties around a bit of the crumbled cheese.
Fry in the oil 3-4 minutes per side until golden and crisp.
steps_detailed: Mash the boiled potato with the achiote paste and finely chopped onion until smooth and evenly colored a warm golden hue.
Take a portion of the mashed potato, flatten it in your palm, place a spoonful of crumbled queso fresco in the center, and fold the potato around it to seal.
Flatten gently into a patty shape, about 1/2 inch thick.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the patties 3-4 minutes per side until golden brown and crisp on the outside.
Serve warm, traditionally alongside grilled meat and a peanut sauce.

name: Ecuadorian Patacones con Queso
steps: Fry the plantain slices until just golden, then flatten each piece.
Fry again until crisp, then top with the crumbled cheese while still hot so it softens.
Serve warm.
steps_detailed: Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the thick plantain slices 2-3 minutes per side until just golden.
Remove and flatten each piece firmly with the bottom of a glass or plate until about half its original thickness.
Stir the minced garlic into the frying oil briefly, then return the flattened plantains to the pan and fry again, 2-3 minutes per side, until deeply golden and crisp.
Drain briefly, then top with the crumbled queso fresco while hot so it softens slightly.
Serve warm as a snack or side.

name: Ecuadorian Espumilla
steps: Whip the egg white until foamy, then gradually add the sugar and whip until thick and glossy.
Fold in the papaya nectar for fruity flavor and color.
Chill before serving in small glasses.
steps_detailed: Whip the egg whites with the lime juice until foamy, then gradually add the sugar, continuing to whip until thick, glossy, and holding soft peaks.
Gently fold in the papaya nectar (traditionally made with guava or other tropical fruit pulp) until evenly incorporated and lightly colored.
Spoon into small glasses and chill at least 20-30 minutes before serving - it's a light, airy, fruity meringue-style treat.

name: Ecuadorian Dulce de Higos
steps: Simmer the figs with the panela and a splash of water for 20 minutes until soft and syrupy.
Serve warm or at room temperature with a bit of queso fresco alongside.
steps_detailed: Combine the figs, panela, cinnamon, and 1/2 cup water in a small saucepan.
Simmer gently for 20-25 minutes until the figs are very soft and plump and the liquid has reduced to a thick, glossy syrup.
Let cool slightly and serve warm or at room temperature with a small piece of queso fresco alongside - the sweet-salty pairing is traditional.

name: Ecuadorian Torta de Choclo Dulce
steps: Blend the corn kernels with the egg, sugar, and melted butter until mostly smooth.
Pour into a small greased baking dish.
Bake at 350F for 30-35 minutes until set and golden on top.
steps_detailed: Blend the corn kernels with the eggs, sugar, melted butter, flour, baking powder, and cinnamon until you have a mostly smooth, slightly textured batter.
Pour into a small greased baking dish and smooth the top.
Bake at 350F (175C) for 30-38 minutes until set, golden on top, and a toothpick comes out mostly clean.
Let cool slightly before slicing - it's dense and moist, a bit like a cross between cornbread and cake.

name: Ecuadorian Aji Picante
steps: Combine the tomatoes, jalapeno, and cilantro in a bowl.
Stir in the lime juice and season with salt.
Let sit 10-15 minutes before serving alongside almost any dish.
steps_detailed: Combine the diced tomatoes, jalapeno, cilantro, and onion in a bowl.
Stir in the lime juice and season generously with salt.
Let sit at room temperature 15-20 minutes so the flavors meld.
Serve as the everyday table condiment alongside soups, empanadas, or grilled meats.

name: Ecuadorian Salsa de Mani

ingredients.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Whisk the peanut butter with the milk and achiote paste until smooth and pourable.
Warm gently in a small saucepan.
Serve over llapingachos or grilled meats.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Saute the onion and garlic briefly in a small pan until just softened.
Whisk in the peanut butter, milk, and achiote paste, stirring until completely smooth.
Warm gently over low heat for 3-4 minutes, stirring, until slightly thickened - don't let it boil hard or it may separate.
Serve warm, spooned generously over llapingachos, grilled meats, or boiled potatoes.

name: Ecuadorian Curtido de Cebolla
steps: Rinse the sliced onion briefly under cold water, then toss with the lime juice and diced tomato.
Season with salt and let sit 15 minutes before serving.
steps_detailed: Rinse the sliced red onion briefly under cold water to soften its bite, then pat dry.
Toss with the lime juice, diced tomatoes, and cilantro, and season well with salt.
Let sit at room temperature 15-20 minutes so the onion pickles slightly and softens.
Serve as a bright, tangy condiment alongside ceviche-style dishes or grilled meats.
